metadata.description: Key physical examination maneuver for anterior shoulder instability.
Anterior cuff tendon providing internal rotation and anterior stability.
More mobile lateral meniscus with popliteus hiatus; commonly torn with ACL injuries.
Distal biceps insertion on the radial tuberosity enabling elbow flexion and supination.
Superior labrum and biceps anchor complex at the glenoid origin.
Posterior cuff tendon providing external rotation strength.
Primary lateral knee stabilizer resisting varus stress.
Superior rotator cuff tendon initiating abduction and commonly torn in cuff pathology.

metadata.claim_text: Students often underweight exam findings and over-rely on imaging when evaluating anterior shoulder instability.

metadata.description: Posterolateral knee stabilizing complex including LCL, popliteus, and arcuate ligaments.

metadata.claim_text: Shoulder instability is usually a labral/capsular injury pattern whose recurrence risk is highest in young active patients.
Anterior Shoulder Instability pathoanatomy centers on labrum, glenoid, humeral-head and related stabilizers.
Do not anchor on a single exam maneuver for anterior shoulder instability — integrate mechanism, instability symptoms, and imaging pattern.
